policy_statement: Singapore pledged to reduce emissions intensity by 36% from 2005 levels by 2030 and stabilise emissions with the aim of peaking around 2030.
verbatim_text: In 2015, building on our earlier commitment, Singapore pledged to reduce our Emissions Intensity (EI) by 36 per cent from 2005 levels by 2030, and stabilise emissions with the aim of peaking around 2030.
section_header: SINGAPORE'S COMMITMENTS TO ADDRESS CLIMATE CHANGE
extraction_rationale: Formal pledge with numeric EI target and timeframe; reiterated national commitment.
reasoning: The verbatim text contains a quantified mitigation outcome (“reduce our Emissions Intensity (EI) by 36 per cent from 2005 levels”) with an explicit deadline (“by 2030”), and it is framed as a national “pledged” commitment. It also includes a secondary, weaker element (“aim of peaking around 2030”) that is less precise, but the primary EI target remains measurable and time-bound. No explicit binding legal mechanism (e.g., law/mandate) is stated in the excerpt.
validation_reasoning: Quantifiable target: Yes — the text states “reduce our Emissions Intensity (EI) by 36 per cent from 2005 levels”. Timeline: Yes — it specifies “by 2030”. Binding mechanism: No — the excerpt describes a “pledged” commitment but does not cite an enforceable instrument (law/regulation/mandate). Spatial specificity: Yes — it applies to “Singapore”. Weak language: Yes — it includes “with the aim of peaking around 2030”, which is aspirational and uses “around”. Strong language: Yes — “pledged to reduce… by 36 per cent… by 2030” is a firm, numeric commitment. STRICT VALID RULE: (1) Auditable commitment met via EI reduction metric; (2) Gold standard met because timeline and quantifiable target are both present; (3) Language-strength rule satisfied because despite weak language in the peaking clause, the presence of a clear numeric target with deadline supports validity even without an explicit binding mechanism in the excerpt.

Binding mechanism: No — it is described as a "pledged" action, with no indication of an ordinance, regulation, mandate, or enforceable requirement in the excerpt. Strong language: No — no mandate/require language is used. Despite weak language, the strict rule passes because it meets the gold standard (timeline + quantifiable target) and the commitment is auditable as stated.

policy_statement: Land Transport Master Plan: Singapore will expand rail network to 360 km by 2030 and add more than 120 trains, increasing total fleet size by more than 40%.
verbatim_text: By 2030, the rail network will be 360km, and will be comparable in rail density with developed cities like London, New York and Tokyo. More than 120 trains will also be added to existing train lines, increasing the total fleet size by more than 40 per cent.
extraction_rationale: Quantified infrastructure expansion commitments with timeframe (2030) and fleet increase.
reasoning: The statement is an actionable, auditable infrastructure commitment in the transport sector. The verbatim text contains an explicit deadline (“By 2030”) and multiple measurable outcomes (rail network length to “360km”; “More than 120 trains” added; fleet size increase “by more than 40 per cent”). The language is strong (“will be”, “will also be added”) rather than aspirational. Spatial specificity is implicit to Singapore’s rail network (national scope), which is sufficient for “where it applies.”
validation_reasoning: Quantifiable targets: the text specifies measurable outcomes: "the rail network will be 360km" and "More than 120 trains will also be added" and "increasing the total fleet size by more than 40 per cent." Timeline: explicit deadline given as "By 2030". Binding mechanism: no explicit ordinance/mandate/regulatory code is cited; it is a plan commitment, so marked No. Spatial specificity: applies to Singapore’s rail network (national/city-state scope) as indicated by context and the statement about rail density comparisons; thus it is location-specific. Weak language: none detected; phrasing is definitive ("will be", "will also be added"). Strong language: yes, definitive verbs with hard numbers ("will be 360km", "More than 120 trains", "more than 40 per cent"). Meets STRICT VALID RULE: auditable deliverables + has_timeline=Yes and has_quantifiable_target=Yes; weak_language=No so language-strength criterion satisfied even without binding mechanism.

policy_statement: Singapore will quadruple sheltered walkways from 56 km to 200 km by 2018; expand cycling paths to more than 700 km by 2030; and require private developers (from July 2016) to consider pedestrians’ and cyclists’ needs in development plans.
verbatim_text:
To facilitate walking and cycling, our sheltered walkway network will be quadrupled from the existing 56km to 200km by 2018. Cycling paths will double from 355km in 2015 to more than 700km by 2030.

From July 2016, LTA and the Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA) will require private developers to consider the safety, convenience, and accessibility needs of pedestrians and cyclists in their development plans.
extraction_rationale: Multiple quantified infrastructure targets plus a dated regulatory requirement (from July 2016).
reasoning: The statement is an actionable, measurable transport policy package. The verbatim text specifies two infrastructure expansion outcomes with clear baselines and deadlines (200 km sheltered walkways by 2018; >700 km cycling paths by 2030) and adds a binding regulatory requirement effective July 2016 for private developers to account for pedestrian/cyclist needs in development plans. This satisfies the strict criteria (quantifiable targets + timelines, and strong language with an explicit requirement).
validation_reasoning: Quantifiable targets: (1) "sheltered walkway network will be quadrupled from the existing 56km to 200km" and (2) "Cycling paths will double from 355km in 2015 to more than 700km" provide measurable outcomes in km with baselines. Timeline: explicit deadlines "by 2018" and "by 2030" are stated, and an effective date "From July 2016" is provided for the developer requirement. Binding mechanism: enforceable language appears in "LTA and the Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA) will require private developers to consider..." indicating a mandate on developers. Spatial specificity: the excerpt does not explicitly state a jurisdictional scope like "Singapore" or "nationwide" within the verbatim text itself (it implies it via agencies, but not explicitly), so marked No. Weak language: no purely aspirational verbs (e.g., encourage/explore/aim) are used; instead it uses "will be" and "will require" with hard numbers. Strong language: "will be quadrupled" and "will require" paired with numeric targets and dates indicates strong, auditable commitments. Therefore the policy meets the strict valid rule (timeline Yes + quantifiable target Yes, and no weak language / binding mechanism present).

It is an auditable infrastructure outcome (not just a study or aspiration), applies implicitly jurisdiction-wide (not a specific neighborhood), and uses strong delivery language (“will be built”).

Binding mechanism: no explicit ordinance/mandate/regulation or legally enforceable instrument is named; it is a commitment statement only -> No. Spatial specificity: no location or jurisdictional scope (e.g., citywide, specific districts) is stated -> No. Weak language: no terms like "encourage", "explore", "aim" appear -> No. Strong language: "will be built" indicates firm delivery intent tied to a hard number and date -> Yes. STRICT VALID RULE: auditable commitment satisfied (build 7 hubs); gold standard satisfied (timeline Yes + quantifiable target Yes); language strength satisfied (weak language No) -> VALID.

policy_statement: These measures aim to increase public transport mode share during morning peak hours to 75% by 2030 and 85% by 2050.

extraction_rationale: Quantified mode share targets with time horizons (2030, 2050).
reasoning: The statement is measurable and time-bound: it specifies public transport mode share targets (75% by 2030; 85% by 2050) for morning peak hours, making it auditable. However, the wording uses “aim,” which is weak/non-binding language and there is no explicit binding mechanism described (e.g., mandate, ordinance). Under the strict rule, it still qualifies as VALID because it has both a quantifiable target and a timeline, and weak language is acceptable when the gold-standard measurability criteria are met (though confidence is moderated due to lack of enforceability details).
validation_reasoning: Quantifiable target: Yes — specifies mode share values: "75 per cent by 2030" and "85 per cent" (by 2050). Timeline: Yes — explicit deadlines "by 2030" and "By 2050". Binding mechanism: No — text only says "aim" and does not reference a requirement/mandate/regulation. Spatial specificity: No — no location/jurisdiction stated (only "during morning peak hours"). Weak language: Yes — "collectively aim to increase" and "the aim is" indicate aspiration rather than obligation. Strong language: No — no mandatory verbs (e.g., require/mandate) accompany the targets. STRICT VALID RULE: Passes because it is an auditable commitment with both timeline and quantifiable targets; despite weak language, the commitment remains measurable.
